words.etymology: From Old Georgian ალქატი (alkaṭi), from Old Armenian աղքատ (ałkʻat).

words.etymology: Borrowed from English antiparticle. Surface analysis yields ანტი- (anṭi-) + ნაწილაკი (nac̣ilaḳi, “particle”).
From არა- (ara-, “un-, non-”) + კომერციული (ḳomerciuli, “profit, commercial, mercantile”).

words.en: place where goods are very expensive
glosses: place where goods are very expensive
words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian апте́ка (aptéka) undergoing Georgianization to make the word pronounced more freely.

words.etymology: From აღმოცენება (aɣmoceneba) + -ული (-uli, past passive participle-forming suffix).

examples.roman: amaɣam, ak, čemtan šeiq̇rebian da gvekneba baasi liṭeraṭuraze
examples.en: Tonight, here at my place, they will gather and we will have a discussion about literature

words.etymology: Borrowed from Russian банкир (bankir), itself from German Bankier, ultimately from French banquier.
From ბგერათა (bgerata, “of sounds”) + ცვალებადობა (cvalebadoba, “variability, variance, changeability”).
Borrowed from Russian би́ржа (bírža), from German Börse. See there for further etymology.
Via Russian бюджет (bjudžet), ultimately from English budget.
The noun is a substantivization of the future passive participle.

words.etymology: From Proto-Kartvelian *qed-, *qid-, *qd-. Old Georgian ჴიდი (qidi) might be from the same source; see there for more.

words.etymology: From Old Georgian ერთობა (ertoba), ერთობაჲ (ertobay). By surface analysis, ერთი (erti, “one”) + -ობა (-oba, “-hood”).
From Middle Georgian ერკემალი (erḳemali), from Old Armenian երկեամ (erkeam, “of two years”).

Possibly from Persian وزک (vazak), alternative form of وزغ (vazaġ, “toad”), suffixed with -ა.
